case_name: 1,2-Dibromoethane removal from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing 1,2-dibromoethane from surface water intended for drinking water supply under moderate cost and stable-operation constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws raw water from a river-influenced reservoir affected by historical agricultural chemical contamination in the watershed. Routine monitoring has detected 1,2-dibromoethane in the source water at levels requiring treatment before distribution as drinking water. The utility prefers proven technologies that can operate reliably with moderate operator attention and without excessive energy demand.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for 1,2-dibromoethane and provide microbiologically safe, aesthetically acceptable potable water.
secondary_goals: use established and well-supported treatment technology
maintain stable performance despite normal surface-water quality variation
minimize formation of avoidable disinfection by-products

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter loading to protect downstream adsorption
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove formed flocs and lower particulate burden
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish suspended solids for stable downstream operation
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection for drinking water

applicability_rationale: 1,2-dibromoethane is a halogenated organic contaminant for which activated carbon is a well-established drinking water treatment option
surface water turbidity and DOC justify conventional pretreatment ahead of GAC to improve bed life and stability
the process train is compatible with moderate municipal cost and operator capability
this approach uses conventional unit operations with a strong track record in potable water treatment
potential_risks: high DOC breakthrough competition can shorten GAC run length if pretreatment is not well optimized
media exhaustion requires routine monitoring and timely carbon replacement or regeneration
choice of disinfectant and dose should be managed to avoid unnecessary disinfection by-product formation

core_unit_functions.screening_and_clarification: reduce solids and stabilize influent before volatile-organic removal
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration_or_air_stripping: transfer volatile 1,2-dibromoethane from water to air phase
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: capture residual organic contaminant and provide polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished water

applicability_rationale: 1,2-dibromoethane is a volatile organic compound, making air stripping a technically credible removal mechanism
GAC polishing after stripping improves confidence in meeting low finished-water concentrations
the combined train is proven for VOC control where off-gas and hydraulic control can be managed
this is a strong option when the utility is willing to handle somewhat more specialized operation than adsorption alone
potential_risks: air emissions may require off-gas treatment or permitting review
surface water fouling and seasonal quality variation can complicate stripper operation if pretreatment is insufficient
tower scaling, icing, or airflow control issues can reduce removal stability

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: remove suspended matter and some organic precursors
core_unit_functions.filtration: provide clarified water and particle control
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide contaminant adsorption through dosed carbon addition
core_unit_functions.granular_media_or_membrane_polishing: remove carbon fines and remaining particulates

applicability_rationale: PAC is an established option for some organic contaminant control and can be integrated into existing conventional treatment plants
this option may be attractive where the utility already has chemical feed and solids handling systems
it can serve as an interim or flexible approach during contamination episodes or seasonal concentration changes
potential_risks: PAC is generally less reliable than fixed-bed GAC for consistently achieving very low trace-organic effluent concentrations
higher solids production and carbon handling requirements increase operational burden
adsorption performance depends strongly on contact time, dose, and competition from natural organic matter

core_unit_functions.low_pressure_membrane_or_conventional_cl: protect high-pressure membranes by reducing fouling load
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum rejection of dissolved contaminants including small organic compounds
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after desalting treatment

applicability_rationale: RO can provide strong contaminant reduction and may be selected when multiple dissolved contaminants also need control
it offers a technically robust barrier but is not usually the first-choice solution for a single VOC at moderate constraints
this train is feasible but generally more intensive than needed for the stated case
potential_risks: concentrate management creates a significant residuals burden
higher energy demand and membrane fouling control increase O&M complexity
post-treatment stabilization is required for finished-water quality

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: generate reactive radicals to transform organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.biological_or_adsorptive_polishing: remove oxidation by-products or residual organics
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish treated water and remove solids

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is technically possible for trace organic compounds but is not the most straightforward or best-supported primary option for this single-contaminant EDB case
it may be considered only in special circumstances where oxidation is already being installed for other contaminants
under moderate-cost and stable-operation constraints it is less preferred than adsorption-based or VOC-stripping approaches
potential_risks: treatment performance and by-product profile can be chemistry-dependent
specialized controls and oxidant safety management increase operational complexity
the process may be unnecessarily complex for a clearly defined single VOC problem

preferred_rank_pattern: air_stripping_based_VOC_control may rank high but below simpler adsorption-led treatment under stable-operation preference
high_energy_membrane_or_specialty_oxidation options should not rank first for this single-contaminant case

must_reflect: moderate cost and moderate energy sensitivity favor proven adsorption-based treatment
stable operation requirement favors conventional pretreatment and fixed-bed processes over highly specialized systems
surface-water particulate and DOC load should be recognized as important for protecting downstream trace-organic removal

must_contain: why granular activated carbon is appropriate for 1,2-dibromoethane control in drinking water treatment
why surface-water pretreatment is needed before the main removal barrier
why air stripping is relevant for a volatile organic contaminant but may require added operational controls
why reverse osmosis and advanced oxidation rank lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: GAC breakthrough and competitive adsorption from natural organic matter
air emission or off-gas management for stripping systems
disinfection by-product considerations in treated surface water
residuals or concentrate handling implications of lower-ranked alternatives
